name: Extra Array (copy back)
summary: Build a reversed copy in a second array, then write it back over the input. Simple, but it uses O(n) extra memory — exactly what the in-place version avoids.
visualizationIntent: init: the input characters shown as a row; an empty second array 'rev' sits alongside (the extra O(n) memory). build (L5-6): pointer i walks the input from the end; each character is read (compared) and pops into rev, so rev fills up as the visible reversed copy. writeback (L7-8): pointer i walks forward and each s[i] is overwritten (current) with rev[i]. return: s now matches rev — reversed. The learner sees a full second array had to exist, unlike the in-place approach.

lineExplanations.3: Record the length to bound both loops.
lineExplanations.4: Create an empty second array to hold the reversed copy (the extra memory).
lineExplanations.5: Walk the input from the last index toward the first.
lineExplanations.6: Append each character to the copy, building it back-to-front (reversed).
lineExplanations.7: Now walk forward to copy the reversed array back into the input.
lineExplanations.8: Overwrite each input character with the corresponding reversed one.
lineExplanations.9: The input now holds the reversed characters — return it.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def reverseString(self, s): — defines the method; s is the list of characters.
syntaxExplanations.3: n = len(s) — the number of characters.
syntaxExplanations.4: rev = [] — an empty list to build the reversed copy (the extra memory).
syntaxExplanations.5: for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1): — count down from the last index to 0.
syntaxExplanations.6: rev.append(s[i]) — add characters back-to-front, building the reverse.
syntaxExplanations.7: for i in range(n): — count up from 0 to write the copy back.
syntaxExplanations.8: s[i] = rev[i] — overwrite the input with the reversed characters.
syntaxExplanations.9: return s — the input array, now reversed.
